---
title: "The modern inmensity of the urban"
format: 
  html:
    css: styles.css
    page-layout: full
---

::::: {.thesis-container}

::: {.sidebar-title}
# The modern inmensity of the urban

In pre-modern times, we had a relationship with the urban environment that could be addressed, if not manipulated, through locomotion. Wandering was a sufficient mode of transport to experience and intuitively assimilate the urban tissue. The size of the city could be classified, using Freundschuh and Egenhofer’s spaces, as an environmental scale, where the designer could become subject to the different perceptual cues of the city over time

As cities grew larger, their scale began to represent not just a practical complication of transport, but an ontological withdrawal of the urban phenomenon and an epistemological shift toward more abstract ways of conceiving the city. The technological development of transport—cars, trains, and planes— allowed us to transformed the experience of citizens into a discontinuous transit between environments. Although this technological development allowed us to handle and tailor our own individual urban environments within the city, we lost an general aspect of the urban. The passage to a geographic scale meant that we were cognitively surpassed by the urban information (Stolk & Portugali, 2016).

Across the spam of two centuries this change in magnitude widrew the experience of the city from our sensibilities as we knew it. It transformed it into a metropolis, assembled in megalopolis and nested finaly in a ecumenopolis: The phenomena of the urban enviromented enters now partialy in our lifes, closer to climate and other worldly events, it has become psicolgicably increasingly inaccesible in its totality by pure sensible intuition, and the design medium trougth wich we concive them have turn virtual domains. 
What was lost during transformation? How do we did built the modern city if not trougth intuition? Who were technological "winners" and the "loser" of this revolution? Why intuition cannot be convey througt modern design medium?? How could we trace a genalogy of intuitive design mediums?
:::

:::: {.main-content}

<br><br><br><br><br>

***

*figura 1. BULP Ubicaciones de propiedades edificadas [^1]*

::: {.video-container}
```{=html}
<video width="100%" autoplay loop muted playsinline controls>
  <source src="figures/BUPL.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
```
:::

*figure 2. Variación de ubicaciones de propiedades edificadas[^2]*

::: {.video-container}
```{=html}
<video width="700" autoplay loop muted playsinline controls>
  <source src="figures/BUPL_VARIATION.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Evolución de la población por ciudad (Top 5 destacadas)"

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

# 1) Lee el CSV
df = pd.read_csv(
    "data/WUP2018-F11b-30_Largest_Cities_in_2018_by_time_delimitado.csv",
    sep=";", engine="python", decimal=","
)

# 2) Filtra y calcula tasas de crecimiento
years_20 = [1950, 1970, 1990, 2010, 2030]
pivot = (
    df[df['Year'].isin(years_20)]
    .pivot_table(index='Urban Agglomeration', columns='Year',
                 values='Population (millions)')
    .reset_index()
)
pivot['1950-1970'] = (pivot[1970] - pivot[1950]) / pivot[1950]
pivot['1970-1990'] = (pivot[1990] - pivot[1970]) / pivot[1970]
pivot['1990-2010'] = (pivot[2010] - pivot[1990]) / pivot[1990]
pivot['2010-2030'] = (pivot[2030] - pivot[2010]) / pivot[2010]

growth = pivot.assign(**{
    'Mean Growth Rate': pivot[['1950-1970','1970-1990','1990-2010','2010-2030']].mean(axis=1)
}).sort_values('Mean Growth Rate', ascending=False)

# 3) Top 5 y resto como 'Otras'
top5 = growth['Urban Agglomeration'].head(5).tolist()
df['color_group'] = df['Urban Agglomeration'].apply(
    lambda c: c if c in top5 else 'Otras'
)

# 4) Crear figura inyectando Urban Agglomeration en custom_data
fig = px.line(
    df,
    x='Year',
    y='Population (millions)',
    color='color_group',
    line_group='Urban Agglomeration',
    custom_data=['Urban Agglomeration'],
    hover_data={                      # ocultamos todo lo automático
        'color_group': False,
        'Year': False,
        'Population (millions)': False,
        'Urban Agglomeration': False
    },
    labels={
        'Year': 'Año',
        'Population (millions)': 'Población (millones)'
    },
)

# 5) Tooltip personalizado usando customdata[0]
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=
        "Ciudad : %{customdata[0]}<br>" +
        "Año : %{x}<br>" +
        "Población (millones) : %{y:.0f}<br>" +
        "<extra></extra>"
)

# 6) Estilos de fondo blanco
fig.update_layout(
    paper_bgcolor="white",
    plot_bgcolor="white"
)

# 7) Líneas ‘Otras’ en gris claro y top5 más gruesas
for trace in fig.data:
    if trace.name == "Otras":
        trace.update(line=dict(color="lightgrey", width=1))
    else:
        trace.update(line=dict(width=2))

# 8) Mostrar figura
fig
```
![](figures/global_building_atlas_heigth_maps.jpg){#fig-global-atlas fig-cap="Overview of the GlobalBuildingAtlas dataset, consisting of global building polygons, height maps, and LoD1 models.[^1]"}
![](figures/global_building_atlas_heigth_maps.jpg){#fig-gba-overview fig-cap="Overview of the resulting GlobalBuildingAtlas dataset, consisting of global building polygons, building height map, and LoD1 models. Base images © Google Maps.[^1]"}

:::

::::

:::::

:::: {.four-column-container}

::: {.grid-item}
**Column 1 Header**
Insert the text or images for your first column here.
:::

::: {.grid-item}
**Column 2 Header**
Insert the text or images for your second column here.
:::

::: {.grid-item}
**Column 3 Header**
Insert the text or images for your third column here.
:::

::: {.grid-item}
**Column 4 Header**
Insert the text or images for your fourth column here.
:::

::::


[^1]: Uhl, Johannes H.: Animating 200 years of urban spatial development in 35 U.S. cities. Copernicus Publications, 2020. https://doi.org/10.5446/48115
[^2]: Idem.
